05 The Company Purpose - YouTube.mp4
I'll never forget the day I was called out by one of my own employees. I had just finished what I thought was a particularly inspiring all-hands meeting when this 20-something intern, who I barely knew, walks up to me and asks, 'So, why are we doing what we're doing? What's our greater mission and purpose?' Now, what I wanted to say was that our mission was to make money so we could continue paying people like you. But I knew that wasn't the right answer. I mean, aside from it just making me sound like more of an evil capitalistic pig than I already do, I knew it simply wasn't accurate. We do care about our customers. We were excited about the work that we were doing and the value that we were providing.
I just hadn't shared our 'why' with anyone on the team. And for good reason. In the early days, our lone goal at the company was just to stay in business, to survive another day. Then our goal was to actually make some money to prove that we could grow and succeed. But now we were past that. Sales were steady and the team was growing. And while I knew that our selfish business goal was critical, I also knew it wasn't enough to motivate the next generation of team members. Heck. It wasn't really even enough to motivate me. anymore. So. Even though I was blindsided by the question, and a little bit pissed off, if I'm being completely honest, in my heart of hearts, I knew it was the right question at the right time.
In other words, I knew it was a good question that deserved an equally good answer. Unfortunately, I didn't have a good answer to her question, so I just told her the truth. I replied with something along the lines of, 'You know, I'm embarrassed to admit it, but I don't have a great answer to your question.' So let me think about it and get back to you. Now, she probably thought that that would be the end of the conversation, but I took her question seriously and I set out to answer it. So I did some research and I talked to some mentors and advisors who were a few steps ahead of me. And what I learned is that there is a formula for a company. purpose statement.
Now, I'm going to tell you what that formula is, but first let's define what a company purpose statement is. If a three-year target is a selfish, inward-facing, business-centric goal, then the company purpose statement is a selfless, outward-facing, customer-centric goal. This is your why, your reason for existing, and the reason that you and your company would be missed if you were gone. This is the dent that you want to make in your... Corner of the universe. Now, before I give you the formula for crafting your company purpose, I want to point something out that is very, very important. Unlike the three-year target... your company's purpose should not have a time horizon. Now, I know that probably contradicts everything you've heard about setting a mission, but hear me out.
When you think about your company's purpose statement, Don't think of it as a mountain to be conquered. Instead, think of it like the horizon line. If you go outside right now, you should be able to find the horizon line. You might have to climb to the top of a hill or take an elevator to the top floor of a building, but make no mistake— the horizon line is there. It's real. You can see it with your own eyes. It's a place, right? But the thing about a horizon line is that it's always moving. The closer you get— the further it seems to move away from you. Now, I freely admit that this can be super frustrating for a business goal, but it's ideal for a company purpose.
And the fact that it's never truly achieved is exactly the point. Your company purpose shouldn't be a destination. It's the fuel. It moves you forward. It keeps you going. It motivates you to a greater cause. And you never want to run out of fuel, do you? That's why a company's purpose shouldn't have a clear time horizon. Yes, your three-year target needs a time horizon. You might even have a customer-centric goal that has a time horizon. All those things are good. But when it comes to your company purpose statement, think horizon line. Let it be that thing that you get to chase for the rest of your life. The story that you just get to keep telling over and over again.
Okay, so now that you know what a company purpose statement is not, let's talk about what it is and specifically how you can craft one for your business. A company purpose statement combines two things, and those two things are one: contribution, and two: impact. Yep. Contribution. Plus impact. Equals purpose. So let's unpack each term, shall we? Contribution is simply the work you do. I don't want you to overthink this, okay? What do you do and how do you do it? We're not talking about the... results or the value that you deliver. We're simply talking about what you do and how you do it. So again, don't overthink this part. Impact, on the other hand, is the specific value you deliver, the end result, the glorious after.
If contribution is the vehicle, then impact is the destination. Critical that you have both, if your company is going to be able to clearly articulate its purpose. Okay, now that you know the two elements of a company purpose statement— contribution and impact— you're ready for the template itself. All right, here goes. The template for crafting your own company purpose statement is... Two, contribution. So that, impact. Now, I realize that this is a bit cryptic, so let's look at some examples that I think will help drive the point home. Here's what I'm going to do. I'm going to read the company purpose statement, and I want you to see if you can name the company before I reveal it. Sound good? Okay, here's the first one.
To organize the world's information and make it universally accessible and useful. So... Who do you think this is, right? What company is this? Have you figured it out? Well, as I'm sure you've already guessed, it's a little company called Google. Now, in this case, Google fundamentally believes that information should be accessible to everyone. This is the impact that they want to make. And how do they do that? What's their contribution? It's simple. They organize all the world's information so you're never more than a few clicks away from the info you need. This is a massive, massive purpose from one of the most valuable companies in the world. But the formula is the same. Contribution. Plus impact equals purpose. Okay, let's look at another example.
To be the most customer-centric company, a place where customers can find and discover anything they might want to buy online at the lowest possible price. All right, who is this? What tiny little company is this? I'm guessing you probably figured this one out too. It is, of course, Amazon. But let's unpack it. The real contribution is that they have created a store where customers can find anything. That's why they... literally call it the everything store. But why? What does creating a store with a massive selection have to do with fulfilling their greater company purpose? Well, it's simple. Anyone can find anything, if it's all there in one place, then Amazon can create economies of scale, which will create savings for the company that can then be passed on to the consumer.
In other words, the bigger the store, the greater the selection, and the lower the prices, the more customer-centric the company becomes, all of which aligns with the impact they are seeking to achieve— to become the most customer-centric company in the world. Let's look at another one. To connect people to what's important in their lives through friendly air travel. Did you catch that? Not just air travel, but friendly air travel. This is, of course, Southwest Airlines. In this case, there really isn't anything unique about their contribution. So what are they doing, right? What is their contribution? Well, they're connecting people to what's important in their lives. That's just a fancy way of saying... We fly people all around the world. Literally every airline on planet Earth could say the same thing, which is why the airline industry is so commoditized.
They literally all do the exact same thing. Let's take a closer look at their impact. Note that they don't merely connect us to important things, which is something, again, literally anything from another airline to a gas station could say. But they do it through friendly air travel. In this day and age, almost everyone will need to fly on an airplane at some point in their lives, so Flight itself is not that impactful. What is impactful, however, is friendly flight. And anyone who's ever had to catch a red eye out of JFK Airport knows that. So the contribution they make is air travel itself, right? That's that connecting us to what's important, but their impact is friendliness. In flight. Now it's worth pointing out that another company could claim that they could connect people to what's important through luxury air travel, right?
Same contribution. Travel, just a different impact. Luxury versus friendliness. The point is, even in commoditized industries, it is possible to differentiate yourself if you have a clear and compelling company purpose. Okay, let's look at another one. To help humanity thrive by enabling all teams to work together effortlessly. Now, this is from a company called Asana, which sells project management software. And in my opinion, it's a classic example of an eye-rollingly bad statement of purpose. Let's break it down. The contribution is simple enough. They enable teams to work together effortlessly. Okay, they're a project management tool, so I'll buy that one. But... What about their impact? Now, in my not so humble opinion, this is kind of where things go off the rails. Their stated impact is to help humanity thrive.
Now, I'll give it to him. That is a really bold impact statement. Noble, even. But it also feels like it's a bit much, doesn't it? Is it only me? I mean, they're really going to help the whole of humanity thrive by enabling more efficient project management solutions? I mean, I guess it's possible. If the projects people are managing are curing disease or feeding widows and orphans, but this still feels like a bridge too far. I mean, by that same logic, again, gas stations could make the same argument since it provides the fuel that allowed these workers to get to those amazing, humanity-thrive-inducing jobs. And, you know, maybe it's just me. Right, right? Maybe it's just me. Maybe I'm just being a cynical jerk.
Look, I freely admit that I don't know any of the leaders of Asana, nor do I know anyone who works there. So it's totally possible that everyone at that company is fully bought into this purpose statement, but I kind of doubt it. So here's the point that I'm trying to make. Whatever you choose for your company purpose, just make sure it's sincere and believable, right? I'm all for shooting for the stars. Just make sure the people you're trying to motivate— aren't rolling their eyes in cynical disbelief when you proclaim your life-altering vision of the future. Okay, just a couple more examples to really drive this point home. Here's a company with a truly massive purpose, to usher in a post-fossil fuel era so the human race can survive. Yep, you heard that right.
To usher in a post-fossil fuel era, so the human race can survive. This company's impact is nothing less than the survival of the entire human race. And they're doing it by disrupting one of the most successful and entrenched industries in the world. Now. That's a company that would be missed if it were gone. And what's the company that I'm referring to? Well, this one is actually a trick question because it isn't just one company. It's two, and the two companies are Tesla and SpaceX, both incidentally founded by Elon Musk. Now, here's the difference between this company's purpose and the previous one. As bold as it is, it's 100% sincere. Both Elon and everyone else at his... companies believe 100% wholeheartedly in this purpose.
So I don't want you to think that you can't shoot for the stars. In this case, that's literally what they're doing, right? It just needs to be sincere. Okay. Now let's look at one more example, because this is a fun one. To slim your wallet, because clunky, fat wallets are literally a pain in the... Now, I absolutely love this. The company is called Bellroy, and they make wallets. Slim wallets, to be more precise. So, what's their contribution? Simple, it's slim wallets. And what's their impact? Your butt doesn't hurt. They're not solving world hunger. They're not ensuring the survival of the human race. They're just making it more comfortable to sit down. So. Don't think you have to make your company's purpose into some massive world-shaking vision of the future. Just remember our goal: to make a dent in our little corner of the universe. So go claim your dent, however small. Even if your universe happens to be someone else's right butt cheek.
